title: Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Delta Air Lines (DAL) compiled by Gemini 3.1.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A 3.0% growth rate represents modest expansion of cash generation, factoring in Delta's strong market position and massive revenue footprint balanced against the inherent cyclicality and capital intensity of the airline industry.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.0% discount rate accounts for the operational risks and leverage typical of major airlines, as well as macroeconomic sensitivities like fuel price volatility.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2.0% terminal growth rate reflects standard inflation and long-term GDP growth, acknowledging that while Delta is the highest revenue global airline, its mature, capital-intensive business model will likely track broader economic expansion into perpetuity.

thesisParagraphs: Delta Air Lines operates as the premier global carrier, ranking first in revenue and brand value among the world's largest airlines. With over 5,400 daily flights connecting 325 destinations in 52 countries, Delta's operational scale is massive. This extensive network, anchored by its dominant position at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, provides a solid foundation for consistent cash generation.

While the airline industry is notoriously capital-intensive and vulnerable to cyclical shocks, Delta's strategic positioning and strong brand loyalty help mitigate some of these risks. The company generated over $8.3 billion in operating cash flow recently, demonstrating its significant earnings power when travel demand is robust. At its current valuation, the market appears to be overly discounting this cash flow potential relative to typical macroeconomic fears.
verdictFooter: Based on latest available SEC filings. Data via EDGAR and Yahoo Finance (Mar 2026).

faq.question: Why did Gemini pick a 3% growth rate for Delta?
faq.answer: A 3% growth rate assumes steady, incremental expansion for Delta. While it leads the industry in revenue, airlines are highly mature businesses where rapid, sustained cash flow growth is difficult to achieve due to high capital expenditure requirements for fleet modernization.

faq.question: What discount rate was used for Delta's DCF?
faq.answer: A 9% discount rate was selected. This relatively high rate reflects the inherent risks of the airline industry, including sensitivity to oil prices, economic downturns, and intense competition, which increase the cost of capital.

faq.question: How did Gemini calculate Delta's Free Cash Flow?
faq.answer: Free Cash Flow was estimated by taking Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities (approximately $8.34B) and subtracting cash used for capital expenditures, arriving at an estimated FCF base of roughly $8.16B to project forward.
